title: SEGMENT 12 — The pipeline — Daiichi Sankyo, ADC Therapeutics, Pfizer

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The pipeline — Daiichi Sankyo, ADC Therapeutics, Pfizer
Let's step back from chemistry and look at the industry.
Who's making these drugs, who's making money, and what does the pipeline look like?
Because the ADC space, in 2024 and 2025, is one of the most active areas of pharma — both in clinical development and in M&A activity.
The dominant player is Daiichi Sankyo, a Japanese company that historically wasn't even a major oncology player.
Daiichi developed the DXd payload and the tetrapeptide linker that powers T-DXd, and they have built an entire pipeline of ADCs around that platform.
Beyond T-DXd, they have datopotamab deruxtecan — an anti-TROP2 ADC with the same DXd payload, currently in late-stage trials for lung cancer and breast cancer.
They have patritumab deruxtecan — an anti-HER3 ADC, also with DXd, in development for lung cancer.
They have ifinatamab deruxtecan, ireltrutamab deruxtecan, and others — basically the same payload-linker platform attached to different antibodies against different targets.
Each of these is potentially a multi-billion-dollar drug if it works.
In 2023, Daiichi signed a partnership with Merck — the U.
S. one, the one that makes Keytruda — that included up to twenty-two billion dollars in payments for the rights to co-develop three of these ADCs.
Twenty-two billion.
Some of that is upfront, much of it is contingent on regulatory and commercial milestones.
But the headline number tells you how the industry values the Daiichi platform.
Pfizer entered the ADC space aggressively through acquisition.
In 2023 they bought Seagen — the company we mentioned earlier, the one that developed Adcetris and the MMAE-vc platform — for forty-three billion dollars.
Seagen brought to Pfizer not just Adcetris, but also enfortumab vedotin (Padcev, for urothelial cancer), tisotumab vedotin (Tivdak, for cervical cancer), and a pipeline of additional MMAE-based ADCs.
Pfizer is now one of the two biggest ADC companies in the world, partly through internal development of agents like Mylotarg's successor and partly through the Seagen acquisition.
AstraZeneca is the other major Western player, primarily through its partnership with Daiichi Sankyo.
AstraZeneca co-promotes T-DXd, and they have invested heavily in building out the ADC franchise.
They also have internal programs.
Roche has T-DM1 (Kadcyla) and polatuzumab vedotin (Polivy) and a pipeline of follow-on ADCs.
They're a major player, though their pipeline has been slower to advance than Daiichi's.
Gilead acquired Immunomedics in 2020 for twenty-one billion dollars, primarily to get sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy), which we'll discuss in detail in Segment 13.

ADC Therapeutics is a Swiss-American company focused specifically on ADCs, currently best known for loncastuximab tesirine (Zynlonta), an anti-CD19 ADC with a PBD payload, approved for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
They have a pipeline of other PBD-based ADCs and a more focused, single-technology platform than the big pharma players.
Smaller market cap, more concentrated risk, but a real participant in the space.
There are also Chinese companies that have become major ADC developers in the past few years.
RemeGen has disitamab vedotin, an anti-HER2 ADC approved in China.
Sichuan Kelun Biotech signed a multi-billion-dollar deal with Merck for the rights to several ADCs in their pipeline.
The Chinese ADC industry is producing molecules at a rapid clip and licensing them out to Western companies for global development.
The pace of M&A in the space is striking.
In the last few years, we've seen Pfizer-Seagen at $43 billion, Gilead-Immunomedics at $21 billion, Merck-Daiichi at $22 billion in committed payments, AbbVie-ImmunoGen at $10 billion.
Hundreds of millions in upfront payments for early-stage assets.
The collective investment in ADCs over the last five years is somewhere between fifty and a hundred billion dollars, depending on how you count.
Why all this activity?
Two reasons.
First, the clinical results are real — T-DXd is genuinely transforming breast cancer outcomes, and the technology has expanded to other indications.
Second, the patent cliffs facing big pharma in the next few years are enormous.
Keytruda's patent expires in 2028.
Revlimid's already gone.
Many other multi-billion-dollar drugs are coming off patent.
Pharma companies need new growth engines, and ADCs look like one of the most promising.
The pipeline is broad.
Antibodies against TROP2, HER3, B7-H3, CEACAM5, NaPi2b, Nectin-4, MUC16, CD19, CD22, CD37, CD79b, and a dozen other targets are in various stages of clinical development as ADC components.
The combinatorial space is enormous — any of these antibodies times any of several payload classes times any of several linker designs times any of several conjugation chemistries.
We are at the beginning of a massive expansion of the ADC repertoire, not the end of it.
That's the industrial landscape.
Now let's go back to specific drugs and look at one I haven't yet discussed in detail — Trodelvy, the TROP2 ADC.
